denial_pattern_sources: wrong_benefit_category: CMS Essential Health Benefits Bulletin 2022; documented in Aetna member complaints
missing_iro_notice: CMS External Appeal Regulations 45 CFR § 147.136; ACA § 2719
denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter confirms the outcome of our review of the request for Adalimumab (Humira) for psoriasis related to Moderate plaque psoriasis (L40.0).

After clinical review using Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletins (CPBs) and InterQual criteria, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: Step therapy demands methotrexate trial before biologic. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria (MCG guidelines, current edition not specified) for medical necessity determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Insurer says patient must try cheaper alternative X first.

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize Adalimumab (Humira) for psoriasis. If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

Aetna Medical Management

Please note that standard criteria apply regardless of prior submissions.
clinical_context:
The 40-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Moderate plaque psoriasis (L40.0). She has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended Adalimumab (Humira) for psoriasis as the next appropriate step.

Methotrexate stopped for hepatotoxicity; documentation submitted. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on step therapy missing without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.
